sections.content: Latin verbs pack a lot of information into their endings. The ending tells you who is doing the action (I, you, he, we, they) without needing a separate pronoun. In prayers, you will encounter mostly 'we' forms (laudámus — we praise), commands (ora — pray!), and 'I' forms (credo — I believe).

sections.title: The Person Endings
sections.content:
These endings attach to every Latin verb:

-o / -m = I (first person singular)
• Credo = I believe
• Confíteor = I confess

-s = you (singular)
• es = you are

-t = he/she/it
• est = he is / it is
• habet = he has

-mus = we
• Laudámus = we praise
• Adorámus = we adore
• Benedícimus = we bless

-tis = you (plural)
• estis = you (all) are

-nt = they
• sunt = they are

sections.title: Commands (Imperative)
sections.content:
Prayers are full of commands — asking God or the saints to do something:

Singular commands (to one person):
• Ora = Pray!
• Miserére = Have mercy!
• Salva = Save!
• Líbera = Deliver!
• Exáudi = Hear!

Plural commands (to more than one):
• Oráte = Pray! (all of you)
• Veníte = Come! (all of you)

In the Mass:
• 'Ora pro nobis' = Pray for us (command to a saint)
• 'Miserére nobis' = Have mercy on us (command to God)
• 'Líbera nos a malo' = Deliver us from evil

sections.title: Key Verbs in Prayers
sections.content:
These verbs appear in almost every traditional prayer:

• esse (to be): sum (I am), es (you are), est (he is), sumus (we are), sunt (they are)
• oráre (to pray): oro (I pray), ora (pray!), orámus (we pray)
• credere (to believe): credo (I believe)
• laudáre (to praise): laudámus (we praise), laudem (that I may praise)
• adoráre (to adore): adorámus (we adore)
• dare (to give): da (give!), dona (grant!)
• veníre (to come): veni (come!), advéniat (may it come)
• facere (to make/do): fiat (let it be done), fecit (he made)
Person endings: -o/m (I), -s (you), -t (he), -mus (we), -nt (they). Commands end without a person ending: ora, miserere, dona. Recognize these patterns and the verbs in prayers become transparent.
